title: Paper clay house number plaque
subtitle: A flat plaque with incised or relief house numbers, sealed for short outdoor display
excerpt: A small wall plaque with the house number incised into the surface or built up in low relief. Paper clay is the working material because the cellulose fibres reduce cracking around the numeral cuts. Sealed with two coats of exterior varnish for short outdoor display in a sheltered position. Expect a season or two of outdoor life before re-coating or moving indoors.
sourceNotes: Incised lettering and relief numerals on ceramic plaques are documented across European architectural ceramics. V&A open-access collection for plaque-form reference. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Clay with cellulose fibres mixed in, which strengthens joins and thin sections and reduces cracking.
Decoration cut into the surface of the clay rather than built up on top of it.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and carve cleanly.
Air-dried but unfired. For paper clay, the finished structural state.

content.text: A small wall plaque, roughly 18 cm by 12 cm, with the house number cut into the front or built up in low relief.

is the working material here: the cellulose fibres bridge the thin sections around the numeral cuts and reduce cracking as the plaque dries. Sealed with exterior varnish, the plaque takes a season or two of outdoor display in a sheltered position; longer than that and the air-dry body softens.

attrs.title: Air-dry clay outdoors
attrs.body: Paper clay is not weatherproof even when sealed. Hang under an overhang or in a porch rather than full weather, and bring the plaque in during heavy rain or freezing weather. Re-coat or move indoors once the surface softens.

items.name: Printed numeral templates at 60 mm tall
Drinking straw or piping nozzle (4 mm)
Waxed cord or stainless steel wire

content.text: Wedge the paper clay for two minutes on the board. Print the house number at 60 mm tall in a clear typeface, sized to fit comfortably within the plaque with a 30 mm border all around. Cut the numerals out of the printed sheet to use as templates.

Pat the clay into a thick rectangle and lay the guide rails on either side. Roll to an even 8 mm thickness. The plaque is built thicker than a decorative slab because it takes the weight of itself plus wind load when hung outdoors. The rolled slab should measure about 22 cm by 16 cm.

Lay the 18 cm by 12 cm card template on the slab and cut around it with the fettling knife held vertical. The corners can be square, or trimmed at 45 degrees for a softer outline. Run the rib along each cut edge to compress and smooth.

(cut into the slab) or low relief (built up on the slab). Incised numbers read cleanly at a distance and are simpler to execute; relief numbers cast a small shadow that can read more strongly on a textured wall.

Lay the numeral templates on the slab and trace the outlines with the needle tool: a shallow score line only. Lift the templates. Re-trace each outline with the fettling knife held at a slight angle, cutting a V-shaped groove about 4 mm deep into the slab. Smooth any rough edges on the cuts with a damp finger or a fine modelling tool. The groove will fill with shadow once the plaque is mounted, which gives the numbers their definition.

Roll a separate small slab to 5 mm thickness. Cut numerals from this smaller slab using the templates and a craft knife, working slowly. Score the back of each numeral and the spot on the plaque where it will sit, brush both with slurry, and press each numeral on. Smooth the lower edges of each numeral into the plaque with a fingertip so the form rises smoothly out of the surface rather than sitting on it as a separate piece.

Press the drinking straw straight through the slab at two points: each 15 mm down from the top edge, 20 mm in from each side. Two holes hang more stably than one. Twist the straw gently as it comes back out. Pierce while the clay is still soft; piercing leather-hard clay chips the edges.

Set the plaque face-up on the board. Cover loosely with plastic for the first 24 hours so the relief or incised cuts dry at the same rate as the surrounding slab. Once the surface feels firm, weight the plaque flat with a clean tile or board across the back for the next 24 hours: this prevents the warping that flat slabs do when one face dries faster than the other. Remove the weighting once the plaque reaches
takes three to five days at this thickness.

Once bone-dry, sand the edges and the back with 220-grit sandpaper. For incised numbers, paint the inside of each groove with a contrasting acrylic colour (a deep navy or black against a pale slab reads strongly from the street). For relief numbers, paint the raised numerals in a contrasting colour and leave the background plain. Seal the whole plaque, front and back, with three coats of exterior acrylic varnish. The back coat matters: damp absorbs through an unsealed back faster than through a sealed front.

Thread the waxed cord or stainless steel wire through the two pierced holes from the front to the back and tie a single loop at the back, leaving 15 cm of slack between the two holes. Hang from a hook or nail in a sheltered position: under a porch overhang, beside a front door, or on a fence inside a covered porch. Avoid full weather exposure.

Bring the plaque in during heavy rain or freezing weather. Expect one to two seasons of outdoor life before the surface softens; re-coat with a fresh layer of exterior varnish each spring, or move the plaque to an indoor display once the surface begins to chip.
Add the house name in smaller incised lettering below the number.
Press a leaf or fern into the slab corners before you add the numbers. This makes a simple border.
Build a matching small plaque with a flat clay leaf or initial as a paired sign by the door.

items.cause: One face dried faster than the other
items.fix: Weight the plaque flat with a clean tile or board across the back for the first 24 hours; press the warp gently back while still leather-hard

items.symptom: Cracks appear around the incised cuts
items.cause: Plain air-dry clay used rather than paper clay, or drying ran too fast
items.fix: Use paper clay; cover loosely with plastic for the first 24 hours; press fresh paper clay slurry into any small cracks before they widen

items.symptom: Relief numbers lift off the slab
items.cause: Score-and-slip was inadequate, or the back of the numeral was not scored
items.fix: Score both the slab and the back of each numeral deeply with the needle tool, and brush a thick slurry across both faces before pressing

items.symptom: Plaque softens after one season outdoors
items.cause: Single coat of standard PVA used rather than exterior-rated varnish, or full weather exposure
items.fix: Apply three coats of exterior acrylic varnish front and back; hang under an overhang or in a porch rather than full weather
